name: Working Memory Capacity Test
description: Test the limits of your working memory. Show a sequence of numbers: 7, 3, 9, 4, 1. Look away. Recall them. Repeat with longer sequences until you can't hold them. Discover your capacity (usually 5-9 items). Then learn chunking: group items into meaningful patterns (7394 and 1) and you can hold more. This reveals why long explanations are hard to follow and why chunking is powerful.
materials: Numbers written on cards or displayed on screen
Pen and paper for writing responses
Optional: math problems or words to test chunking
learning_objectives: Understand the limits of working memory (5-9 items)
Experience how chunking increases capacity
Recognize situations where you exceed working memory limits
Apply chunking strategies to manage cognitive load
variations: Repeat with words or images instead of numbers
Test with and without distraction (noise, video) to see attention effects
Apply to real studying: break MATH-101 formulas into meaningful chunks

name: Memory Palace Construction
description: The memory palace (or method of loci) is an ancient technique used by scholars and performers. Mentally walk through a familiar place (your home, school, route to the store). Associate information you want to remember with specific locations. 'I'll put the photosynthesis equation by the front door, the water cycle in the living room...' Walk through mentally to retrieve. This demonstrates how spatial memory is powerful and how encoding strategy (vivid, spatial associations) affects recall.
materials: Information to remember (could be MATH-101 formulas, SCI-101 concepts, READ-101 vocabulary)
learning_objectives: Understand that memory encoding is stronger when vivid and spatial
Apply the memory palace technique to information you're learning
Appreciate how organization and association improve retrieval
Transfer spatial memory to multiple subjects
variations: Use a room in your home, a familiar route, or a map of your school
Build progressively: start with 5 locations, expand to 20
Apply to content from other packs: CHEM-101 atomic structure, HIST-101 timeline, CODE-101 algorithm steps

name: Forgetting Curve Tracking: Personal Memory Decay
description: Ebbinghaus discovered the 'forgetting curve' — knowledge decays predictably over time. But strategic retrieval rebuilds memory stronger. Create a list of 20 facts from a pack you're studying (MATH-101, SCI-101, READ-101). Test yourself immediately (100% recall expected). Then test yourself after 1 day, 3 days, 1 week, 2 weeks. Graph the decay. Then study the forgotten items and test again. You'll see: forgetting is normal, strategic retrieval overcomes it.
materials: 20 facts from any pack you're studying
Graph paper or digital graphing tool
learning_objectives: Understand the forgetting curve as a natural phenomenon
Experience how knowledge decays without review
Learn that strategic retrieval reverses decay
Apply spaced review to your real studying
variations: Track multiple lists simultaneously (spacing vs. massing)
Compare spacing intervals: review after 1 day vs. 3 days vs. 1 week
Apply to content from PHYS-101 (formulas), CODE-101 (syntax), LANG-101 (vocabulary) — any pack

name: Sleep Impact on Learning: Design and Document an Experiment
description: Sleep consolidates learning. Design an experiment: choose a learning target (MATH-101 problem set, SCI-101 concepts). Study it one evening, get full sleep, test yourself the next day. Then study similar material, get minimal sleep (or study in the morning instead), test yourself. Compare recall. Document: time slept, test performance, what you noticed. This demonstrates that sleep is not wasted time — it's when consolidation happens.
materials: Learning material to study (from any pack)
Test yourself (write answers or solve problems)
learning_objectives: Understand sleep consolidation
Design a simple scientific experiment
Collect and analyze your own learning data
Apply sleep science to your own study schedule
variations: Compare different sleep durations (6h vs. 8h vs. 10h)
Test other variables: sleep timing (night vs. afternoon nap), caffeine, exercise
Document in a learning journal

name: Retrieval Practice Quiz Generator
description: Retrieval practice is the most powerful learning strategy. But you can't retrieve what you haven't studied. Choose content from any pack (MATH-101 formulas, SCI-101 concepts, READ-101 vocabulary, CRIT-101 fallacies). Create 10 quiz questions that force retrieval without giving away the answer. Take the quiz yourself. The difficulty of retrieval strengthens memory more than reading.
materials: Material from any pack you're studying
Notebook or digital tool to write questions
Answers written separately so you can quiz yourself
learning_objectives: Understand that testing yourself beats reading
Create retrieval opportunities in your studying
Experience the difficulty of retrieval as a sign of learning, not failure
Apply retrieval practice to any subject
variations: Use flashcards (digital or physical)
Quiz yourself with a study partner
Use Anki or similar spaced repetition app
Mix retrieval types: fill-in-blank, multiple choice, short answer

name: Flashcard Spacing Schedule Builder
description: Spaced repetition works: review 1-3 days after learning, then 1 week, then 2-3 weeks, then monthly. Design a spacing schedule for material you're learning (MATH-101 formulas, CHEM-101 reactions, LANG-101 vocabulary, CODE-101 syntax). Create flashcards. Review on day 1. Mark which ones are still hard. Review the hard ones on day 3. Review all on day 7. Document what gets retained. Apps like Anki automate this, but building it yourself teaches the principle.
materials: Material from any pack you're studying
learning_objectives: Understand optimal spacing intervals
Build a spaced review schedule
Experience how spacing prevents forgetting
Use apps like Anki to automate spaced repetition
variations: Start with 10 cards, expand to 50+
Compare manual spacing vs. app-based spacing
Track which spacing intervals work best for you
Apply to any pack: PHYS-101, HIST-101, BUS-101, etc.

name: Interleaving vs. Blocking Comparison Study
description: Blocking (solve 10 linear equations, then 10 quadratics, then 10 geometry problems) feels fluent while practicing but produces weak transfer. Interleaving (alternate equation, geometry, quadratic, equation, geometry...) is harder but produces stronger transfer. Design a study session: choose material from MATH-101 or SCI-101. Study half using blocking (feels easier), half using interleaving (feels harder). Test yourself on mixed problems. You'll discover: interleaving is harder while learning but better for retention and transfer.
materials: Math problems, science content, or other subject material
Two study schedules: blocking and interleaving
learning_objectives: Understand the fluency illusion (familiar = understood)
Experience how interleaving is harder but more effective
Learn when to block (initial learning) vs. interleave (mastery and transfer)
Apply to any subject: PHYS-101 forces, CHEM-101 reactions, CODE-101 algorithms
variations: Compare blocking vs. interleaving systematically on the same material
Track which approach leads to better test performance
Discuss why your brain might feel more confident with blocking

name: Elaboration Practice: Connect New Ideas to What You Know
description: Elaboration means connecting new information to existing knowledge. Choose a concept you're learning in any pack (photosynthesis from SCI-101, polynomial equations from MATH-101, historical causation from HIST-101). Ask: 'How is this like something I already know?' 'Why does this work this way?' 'What would happen if...?' These questions force deeper encoding than passive reading. Write connections, draw diagrams, explain to someone else.
materials: Concept from any pack you're studying
Optional: concept map tool or whiteboard
learning_objectives: Understand elaboration as a powerful encoding strategy
Connect new concepts to existing knowledge
Deepen understanding through questioning
Apply elaboration to any subject
variations: Use concept mapping tools (Miro, Obsidian, paper)
Explain the concept to someone else
Draw diagrams showing connections
Connect across multiple packs: how is ecosystems (SCI-101) like a business system (BUS-101)?

name: Fluency Test: Can You Explain Without Looking?
description: Fluency (easy to read) is often mistaken for understanding. Choose material you've studied from any pack. Read it — it feels easy. Now close the book. Explain it without looking. Can you? If not, you have fluency but not understanding. This is the fluency illusion. Doing this test regularly catches gaps in understanding before the exam.
materials: Material you've studied from any pack
Notebook or voice recording to explain
learning_objectives: Distinguish fluency (easy to read) from understanding (can explain)
Catch fluency illusions before exams
Use as a self-assessment tool
Apply to all your studying
variations: Explain to someone else (even more revealing)
Explain at increasing levels of difficulty (basic → complex → teaching someone else)
Try immediately after reading, then after a day (shows retention too)

name: Learning Journal Setup: Reflective Daily Practice
description: Start a learning journal where you reflect on your learning process, not just content. At the end of each study session, write: 'What did I learn? How did I learn it? What was hard? What strategy would help next time? How does this connect to other things I know?' Over time, patterns emerge. You'll notice what strategies work for you, how you respond to difficulty, whether you're building understanding or just familiarity.
materials: Regular time commitment (daily or after each study session)
learning_objectives: Develop metacognitive awareness through reflection
Track patterns in your learning
Identify effective strategies
Build self-regulation
Connect learning across sessions and packs
variations: Use structured prompts or free-form reflection
Include sketches or diagrams
Record audio reflections
Share with a learning partner
Review monthly to see patterns

name: SMART Goal Workshop: Set and Track Learning Goals
description: Vague goals ('get better at math') don't work. SMART goals do: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Instead of 'understand SCI-101,' try 'Explain the water cycle without notes by Friday.' Instead of 'improve at CODE-101,' try 'Write and debug 5 programs using loops by two weeks.' Set goals that are specific enough that you can tell when you've achieved them.
learning_objectives: Understand why vague goals fail
Set SMART goals for your learning
Track progress toward goals
Adjust goals based on progress
Apply to any subject
variations: Set daily, weekly, and monthly goals
Share goals with a learning partner or mentor
Review and adjust goals regularly
Create goals for content from specific packs: MATH-101, READ-101, CODE-101, etc.

name: Productive Failure Challenge: Struggle Deliberately and Learn
description: Struggle is when learning happens. Choose something hard from a pack you're studying (PHYS-101 concepts, PROB-101 complex problems, CODE-101 debugging). Try to solve it without looking at the solution. Fail. Try again. Fail differently. After genuine struggle, look at the solution. You'll learn more from this struggle than from easy problems. The struggle updated your mental model.
materials: Difficult problem or concept from any pack
learning_objectives: Reframe struggle as a sign of learning, not failure
Experience productive failure
Understand how difficulty strengthens learning
Build resilience and growth mindset
Apply to challenging content in any subject
variations: Document your failed attempts before looking at solution
Discuss with others how their attempts differed
Try increasingly difficult problems
Apply across packs: tackle hard problems in MATH-101, CRIT-101, CODE-101, ENGR-101

name: Cross-Pack Connection Map: How Does This Idea Apply Everywhere?
description: Choose a general principle that appears in multiple packs: 'evidence,' 'systems,' 'causation,' 'design,' 'transfer,' 'iteration.' Map how that principle appears across different packs. Example: Evidence appears in SCI-101 (experimental evidence), HIST-101 (primary sources), CRIT-101 (supporting arguments), and READ-101 (author's claims). Understanding evidence at this abstract level means you can recognize and work with evidence anywhere. Create a concept map showing how the principle manifests differently in each context.
materials: Concept map tool (Miro, Obsidian, pen and paper, digital whiteboard)
learning_objectives: Identify abstract principles that transfer across domains
Map concept instances across different contexts
Build transfer ability through explicit connection
Understand how metacognition amplifies learning
Create personal knowledge frameworks
variations: Start with one principle, expand to multiple
Work with a partner and explain connections to each other
Choose principles: systems, causation, design, evidence, iteration, constraints, transfer
Apply this across all 35 packs over time

name: Teach-Back Partner Session: Explain Learning to Someone Else
description: Find a partner who's learning something different (they're studying MATH-101, you're studying READ-101, for example). Explain what you learned to them. They'll ask questions you can't answer, revealing gaps. But more importantly, explaining forces you to organize your thinking, find the essence of what you understand, and transfer it. Teaching is one of the most powerful learning strategies.
learning_objectives: Use teaching as a learning strategy
Identify gaps in understanding through questions
Build communication skills
Deepen understanding through explanation
Transfer concepts across domains
variations: Teach a younger student
Teach a peer learning the same content but needing help
Record your explanation (reveals what's unclear)
Teach using only words (no notes) — forces deep understanding

name: Personal Learning System Design: Build Your Own Integration Framework
description: You're learning from 35 different packs. How does it all fit together? Design your personal learning system. (1) How do you currently learn? What strategies do you use? (2) What works and what doesn't? (3) Based on LEARN-101, what improvements would help? (4) How do the packs connect? What principles run through multiple packs? (5) How will you implement? Tools? Schedule? Support? Document your system. Revisit and refine it monthly.
materials: Optional: tracking template for strategies and progress
learning_objectives: Design a personal learning architecture
Integrate learning from multiple sources
Apply metacognitive awareness to system design
Build coherent knowledge from diverse inputs
Create sustainable learning habits
variations: Start small (2-3 packs), expand gradually
Include time for reflection, spacing, interleaving, elaboration, transfer
Track which system components work and which don't
Update your system based on experience
Share your system with others and get feedback
